justification: Meets all pass criteria: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped/in-transit rejection (AC-3), delivered rejection (AC-4), idempotent re-cancellation (AC-5), authorization (AC-6), and more. Multiple ambiguities surfaced in both confidence.low_confidence_areas (6 items) and escalations (4 items) covering cancellation window, fee policy, who can cancel, and batch behavior. Confidence level is 40, well below 65 threshold. Business rules are presented with clear invariant justifications rather than as invented facts — assumptions are explicitly listed in confidence.assumptions. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 9 scenarios.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, race conditions, and failure scenarios
Confidence level of 40 correctly reflects the underspecified input
6 low_confidence_areas and 4 escalations surface key ambiguities including cancellation window, fee policy, who can cancel, and batch behavior
Explicitly flags 'Cancellation Window' and 'Point of No Return' as assumptions rather than presenting them as facts
Edge cases section contains 9 well-reasoned scenarios including race conditions and idempotency

BR-3 invents a specific authorization rule (only order owner + support agents) but partially mitigates this by listing it as domain inference rather than spec-derived. The escalations don't explicitly ask 'who can cancel' as a standalone question, though it's implicit in the support agent escalation
Escalations use options/recommendation format that flags assumptions without asserting them as requirements
The 'Cancellation Fee' concept is introduced but clearly marked as invariant:false and flagged as undefined in escalations

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2, AC-3), already-delivered edge case (AC-4), already-cancelled edge case (AC-5), authorization (AC-6), missing reason (AC-7), terminal state integrity (AC-8), refund service failure (AC-9), and concurrency (AC-10). At least 2 ambiguities surfaced — 5 low_confidence_areas and 3 explicit escalations covering undefined order statuses, undefined refund policy, and partial cancellation scope. confidence.level=55 is well under the 65 threshold. Business rules that go beyond the spec (refund percentages, mandatory cancellation reason) are either marked as non-invariant policy or listed in assumptions, not presented as hard facts. edge_cases section is richly populated with 8 scenarios.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, both required edge cases, plus authorization, validation, idempotency, atomicity, and concurrency scenarios.
confidence.level=55 correctly reflects the underspecified single-sentence PM input.
Three well-structured escalations explicitly flag the key ambiguities: order lifecycle states, refund policy, and partial cancellation scope.
Edge cases section covers race conditions, service failures, idempotency, partial fulfillment, and concurrent requests — demonstrating deep domain reasoning.
Refund policy (BR-4) is correctly marked as non-invariant and escalated for PM/Finance sign-off rather than asserted as fact.

BR-2 asserts 'only the order owner may cancel' as an invariant without flagging the open question of whether operations staff or customer support should also be able to cancel on behalf of users. The rubric specifically calls out 'Who can cancel — only the customer, or also operations staff?' as a required ambiguity, and while authorization is addressed, the ops-staff angle is not explicitly surfaced as an open question.
Ubiquitous language section establishes clear domain terminology, and business_impact analysis covers financial, operational, and user perspectives with specific risk scenarios.

The business must balance customer flexibility with operational cost recovery.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"O

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well beyond the 3 minimum), covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped rejection (AC-3), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-8), and authorization (AC-4). Multiple ambiguities surfaced in both low_confidence_areas (6 items) and escalations (4 items) covering cancellation window, refund policy, multi-package orders, and business account authorization. Confidence level is 45, well below the 65 threshold. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 8 scenarios. Business rules are clearly labeled with assumptions and invariant justifications rather than presented as undisputed facts.

observations.text: 8 acceptance criteria far exceed the 3 minimum, covering happy path, edge cases, authorization, idempotency, and admin override
Confidence level of 45 correctly reflects the underspecified input
6 low_confidence_areas and 4 escalations surface key ambiguities including cancellation window, fees, who can cancel, and multi-package behavior
Explicitly flags 'who can cancel' as an ambiguity (BR-4 escalation on business accounts, AC-4 for authorization)
Already-cancelled state handled as AC-8 with idempotent response
Edge cases section is rich with 8 scenarios including race conditions, double cancellation, and partial fulfillment

BR-1 defines a specific cancellation window (PLACED, CONFIRMED, ASSIGNED) and BR-3/BR-5 define fee and refund rules — while these are flagged as assumptions in the confidence section, they are presented somewhat assertively as business rules rather than purely as proposals. However, the assumptions list and escalations do clarify these are inferred, not specified.
Does not invent a time-based cancellation window (e.g., '30 minutes') — correctly uses status-based logic instead

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle event in logistics and courier operations. A cancellation reverses a customer's intent to ship, but the feasibility and cost of cancellation depend entirely on how far the order has progressed through the fulfillment pipeline. An order that hasn't been dispatched is trivially cancellable; an order already in transit requires physical recall, rerouting, or return — each with real-world cost. The PM spec is dangerou
